one_line_takeaway: A Hyperledger-Fabric-on-FedAvg framework that logs and certifies machine-unlearning requests for trust in IoT federated learning; class-level unlearning drives target-class accuracy to zero with modest overall accuracy loss and bounded, amortizing blockchain overhead, but the verifiability claim is never empirically tested and no external unlearning baseline is compared.
key_finding: Unlearning is class-specific and precise: after unlearning, accuracy on the targeted Class 0 falls to exactly zero in every configuration while overall accuracy drops only modestly (MNIST 200ep 80.23%->71.35%, MNIST 500ep 87.11%->77.74%, CIFAR-10 2000ep 34.12%->33.21%, CIFAR-10 5000ep 45.92%->42.65%). Blockchain integration adds a fixed ~35 s one-time setup, ~3 s consensus and ~2 s transaction overhead, keeping per-epoch time at 24-26 s; the relative time penalty vs. plain FL shrinks with more iterations (from 66s vs 26s at t=0 to 28038s vs 26000s at t=1999, i.e. ~154% down to ~8% overhead), which the authors read as evidence of scalable, acceptable overhead.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a framework that couples blockchain (Hyperledger Fabric) with federated learning (FedAvg) to make MACHINE UNLEARNING verifiable and trustworthy in IoT settings. The stated novelty is not the unlearning algorithm itself but the integration layer: smart contracts that (1) automate/certify unlearning requests and log every request and action to an immutable ledger, (2) inject differential privacy into gradients before they are published on-chain, and (3) optimize which training-process data is stored so blockchain overhead stays bounded. Two client roles (training clients vs. unlearning clients) plus an aggregating Agent operate via five smart-contract-driven algorithms (Register, Global Model Upload, Model Updating, Model Aggregation, and Machine Unlearning). The unlearning mechanism itself is a rollback/retrain: on a request, the smart contract 'finds the first epoch before this client uploaded their gradient' and the Agent re-derives the global model from that point, then redistributes it. NOTE: the related-work discussion of FedEraser [15], FedRecovery [14], reverse-SGA/EWC [11], TF-IDF channel pruning [13], FLchain [16], and BFLC [18] describes CITED prior systems, not this paper's own contribution or baselines.
threat_model: Adversary = an untrustworthy or lazy service provider / model owner (the FL server) who claims to have honored a user's deletion request but silently skips the unlearning to avoid compute cost or utility loss, leaving the user no way to verify removal. Secondary concerns: on-chain exposure of sensitive information (since any network participant can read the ledger) and IoT data breaches from centralized storage. The blockchain's immutable, transparent ledger plus smart-contract automation is meant to give clients/auditors a tamper-proof, verifiable record that the unlearning request was received and acted on; differential privacy protects gradient confidentiality; data localization limits breach surface. The threat model is described informally in prose (Sections I, IV, VI); there is no formal adversary definition, no cryptographic proof, and no experiment demonstrating the certification actually catches a cheating server.
limitations: 1) No comparison against any external unlearning baseline (FedEraser, FedRecovery, etc. are only cited, never reproduced or benchmarked); the only 'baselines' are the paper's own normal-training and plain-FL variants. 2) The central verifiability/certification claim is argued qualitatively and never tested — no experiment shows the ledger detecting a dishonest server, so the core security promise is unvalidated. 3) The 'unlearning' is effectively rollback-and-retrain from the epoch before the client's contribution; its exactness beyond the single held-out class is not measured (no membership-inference or influence test). 4) Differential privacy is claimed but no epsilon/delta budget, mechanism, or privacy-utility tradeoff is reported. 5) Publishing (DP-)gradients on a ledger readable by all participants is a privacy risk the paper acknowledges only in prose. 6) Absolute CIFAR-10 accuracy is very low (~34-46%), indicating a weak underlying model that makes the accuracy-preservation claim less compelling. 7) Evaluation is single-run with no variance/confidence reporting.

datasets_used: MNIST (handwritten digits; evaluated at 200 and 500 epochs)
CIFAR-10 (60,000 color images, 10 classes; evaluated at 2000 and 5000 epochs)
quantitative_results: MNIST 200 epochs: average accuracy 80.23% (normal) -> 71.35% (post-unlearning); unlearned Class 0 accuracy = 0%
Blockchain network initialization: ~35 s (one-time); consensus overhead ~3 s; average transaction processing ~2 s; per-epoch time 24-26 s (stable with/without unlearning)

Time cost over 1999 iterations (Table I) Normal FL vs Proposed: t=0 26s vs 66s; t=9 260s vs 318s; t=199 5200s vs 5638s; t=1999 26000s vs 28038s (relative overhead ~154% at t=0 shrinking to ~8% at t=1999)
baselines_compared: Normal Federated Learning (plain FedAvg without blockchain) - used only for time-cost comparison in Table I
Normal training vs. training-with-unlearning (same system, internal comparison) - used for loss/accuracy curves
No external / prior machine-unlearning method (e.g., FedEraser, FedRecovery) was reproduced or benchmarked; those appear only in Related Work
